response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I spend hours every day scrolling through social media while I'm at my coffee shop or winding down after work, and honestly, the fake content situation drives me absolutely crazy. At my age, I've seen enough nonsense to know when something smells off, but that doesn't make it any less irritating when my own kids share obviously fabricated stories without checking them first. The whole thing makes me anxious because I never know what to trust anymore. I'm curious by nature, so when I see something interesting, I want to dig into it, but then I realize half the time I'm just wasting energy on complete garbage. What really gets under my skin is how people just accept whatever pops up on their feed. I've learned the hard way to be skeptical about everything, especially those emotional posts designed to get you riled up. When I see something that seems too outrageous or perfectly confirms what I already suspect about the world, that's my signal to stop and question it. I don't have some fancy system or anything because frankly I'm not that organized about it, but I try to check if the source is legitimate or if other people are calling it out as fake. Sometimes I'll ask my kids about it, though they're not always helpful. The anxiety of potentially spreading misinformation myself keeps me from sharing most things, which maybe isn't the worst approach. Living in New York, you encounter all types, and running my own coffee business has taught me that people believe what they want to believe regardless of facts. I've stopped trying to convince others when they're dead set on something false because it just creates conflict and I don't have the patience for arguments. My approach is basically to trust very little, verify when I can be bothered, and keep my mouth shut most of the time. It's not perfect and sometimes I probably miss real news because I'm too suspicious, but at least I'm not out there spreading lies. The whole social media landscape exhausts me emotionally, but I can't seem to stay away from it either.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 87-year-old with high neuroticism (anxious, worried), low agreeableness (skeptical, less trusting), moderate extraversion, and low openness (prefers familiar approaches, less adventurous with new methods). They spend several hours daily on social media, are curious but not highly conscientious (disorganized approach). As a self-employed barista in NYC with three children, they have practical life experience. The tone should be somewhat anxious, skeptical, direct, and occasionally irritable. They would approach fake content with worry and suspicion rather than systematic verification. Their low conscientiousness means they won't have rigorous checking habits. Their curiosity drives initial interest but anxiety prevents sharing. Low agreeableness suggests they won't sugarcoat opinions and will be blunt about others' gullibility.
response_parsed.tone: anxious, skeptical, direct, somewhat irritable, practical, blunt
